tldr: Tadej Pogacar launched a decisive sprint from Isaac del Toro's long lead-out on the Les Angles summit, beating Jonas Vingegaard by 2 seconds. The 10-second stage bonus vs. Vingegaard's 6 seconds cancels exactly the 6-second GC deficit from Stage 1 — Pogacar takes the yellow jersey on stage-win countback.

narrative.headline: Pogacar pockets yellow on the first Pyrenean day as del Toro's selfless lead-out ignites the Les Angles summit.
narrative.body:
Stage 3 covers 195.9 km from Granollers over three Pyrenean climbs — the Col de Toses, the Col du Calvaire, and the final ramp into the ski resort of Les Angles — for the race's first genuine summit finish. With more than an hour of skirmishing, a 19-rider breakaway finally forms including Egan Bernal, Mads Pedersen, Magnus Cort, Luke Plapp, Alex Baudin, Nicolas Prodhomme, and others. Bernal suffers a puncture on the Col de Toses descent and loses contact, ending a promising day for the former Tour winner.

The break's advantage swells to over three minutes before UAE Emirates, led by Florian Vermeersch, starts a controlled reduction. On the Col de Toses, Raúl García Pierna escapes solo; Vlad Van Mechelen and Mathéo Vercher bridge across partway up before Alex Baudin, George Bennett, and Prodhomme join on the upper slopes. Baudin crests the Col de Toses summit first, claiming the polka-dot jersey from Alex Molenaar. On the Col du Calvaire, Baudin attacks again and Prodhomme bridges; at that summit the pair still hold 43 seconds on the now-reduced peloton.

On the descent, Prodhomme sits up and rejoins the main group before the new King of the Mountains is caught by the peloton with 11 km remaining on the final Les Angles ramp. On the 1.7 km summit finish, Isaac del Toro delivers a prolonged lead-out for Pogacar that cracks any lingering attacks. Inside the final 200 metres, Pogacar unleashes his sprint and immediately opens a gap nobody can close. He crosses the line 2 seconds ahead of Vingegaard — Richard Carapaz and Paul Seixas finish in the same time as Vingegaard, with a second group of Tobias Halland Johannessen, Lennert Van Eetvelt, Florian Lipowitz, Remco Evenepoel, Isaac del Toro, and Juan Ayuso arriving 4 seconds back.

With 10 seconds of stage bonus (versus 6 for Vingegaard), Pogacar erases exactly the 6-second GC gap that separated them since Stage 1. The two rivals are level on aggregate time entering Stage 4 — Pogacar leads by virtue of the stage victory and dons yellow for the first time in this Tour.

gcImpact.headline: Pogacar in yellow; he and Vingegaard are identically level on time entering Stage 4.
gcImpact.body: Tadej Pogacar (UAE Team Emirates XRG) wears the yellow jersey. He and Jonas Vingegaard (Team Visma | Lease a Bike) share the same aggregate race time — Pogacar leads on stage-win countback. Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) third at +0:23. Isaac del Toro (UAE) fourth at +0:24. Juan Ayuso (Lidl-Trek) fifth at +0:27. Paul Seixas (Decathlon CMA CGM) sixth at +0:48. Florian Lipowitz (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) seventh at +0:53. Lenny Martinez (Bahrain - Victorious) eighth at +1:09. Tobias Halland Johannessen (Uno-X Mobility) ninth at +1:11. Ilan Van Wilder (Red Bull - BORA - hansgrohe) tenth at +1:17. Tom Pidcock (Pinarello-Q36.5) eleventh at +1:22. Richard Carapaz (EF Education-EasyPost) twelfth at +1:45. Mattias Skjelmose (Lidl-Trek) fifteenth at +1:50 — finishes 11th on the stage at +0:10 but already carried a deficit from the earlier stages. Thymen Arensman (Netcompany-INEOS Cycling Team) sixteenth at +2:19.

riderPerformances.name: Tadej Pogacar
riderPerformances.narrative: Pogacar times his effort perfectly on Les Angles, conserving energy while del Toro sets a punishing lead-out before uncorking a sprint in the last 200 metres that none of the GC group can answer. The 2-second margin over Vingegaard, combined with a 4-second bonus differential, cancels out the 6-second GC deficit from Stage 1 exactly — Pogacar takes the yellow jersey on stage-win countback. His stage win signals that UAE will dictate rather than follow through the Pyrenees.

keyMoments.what: Launches decisive sprint in the final 200 m of Les Angles ramp, gaps all rivals immediately

riderPerformances.name: Jonas Vingegaard
riderPerformances.narrative: Vingegaard matches Pogacar stride for stride over the Col du Calvaire and the Les Angles ramp but is pipped in the sprint, conceding 2 seconds in stage time and 4 seconds in bonus differential — precisely the margin that transfers yellow. He exits Stage 3 level on aggregate time with Pogacar, second only on countback, confirming the Tour remains a two-man contest. His Visma squad kept rivals at bay through the descent to the final climb.

keyMoments.what: Second across the line, 2 seconds behind Pogacar; yellow jersey transfers on countback

riderPerformances.name: Richard Carapaz
riderPerformances.narrative: Carapaz delivers EF Education-EasyPost's best result of this Tour, finishing third on the Les Angles summit at the same time as Vingegaard. The Ecuadorian entered Stage 3 well outside the top 10 GC but climbs ferociously on the final ramp, earning the 4-second bonus and moving to twelfth overall at +1:45. His teammate Baudin also excelled in the breakaway — a standout day for the EF squad in the Pyrenees.

keyMoments.what: Third at the summit finish, same time as Vingegaard, claims 4-second bonus

riderPerformances.name: Remco Evenepoel
riderPerformances.narrative: Evenepoel holds with the second GC group through the Les Angles summit, finishing eighth at +0:04. He moves to third overall at +0:23 — a controlled but watchful performance. Red Bull-Bora kept their two GC leaders Evenepoel and Lipowitz both in the main group, limiting their losses to a handful of seconds on the day's first genuine mountain test.

keyMoments.what: Eighth at summit finish, same time as Lipowitz and del Toro at +0:04; third in GC at +0:23

riderPerformances.name: Florian Lipowitz
riderPerformances.narrative: Lipowitz finishes seventh in the second GC group at +0:04, holding seventh overall at +0:53. Quiet and efficient — he sits in the wheels through the Col du Calvaire and survives the Les Angles finish comfortably. With Evenepoel also in the group, Red Bull-Bora keeps two GC leaders well placed through the first big mountain day.

riderPerformances.name: Isaac del Toro
riderPerformances.narrative: After winning Stage 2, del Toro switches entirely to a domestique role for Stage 3, delivering a sustained lead-out on the final Les Angles ramp that sets the conditions for Pogacar's winning sprint. He finishes ninth at +0:04 — still in the main GC group — and holds fourth overall at +0:24. The 22-year-old's willingness to ride for a teammate immediately after his first Tour stage win underlines UAE's collective depth and tactical discipline.

keyMoments.what: Takes up a long lead-out for Pogacar in the final kilometre, setting pace that cracks any remaining attacks

riderPerformances.name: Mattias Skjelmose
riderPerformances.narrative: Skjelmose finishes 11th at +0:10, holding on closer to the summit group than feared but still unable to follow Pogacar's final surge. He sits 15th in GC at +1:50 after the stage — a gap that leaves him well outside the top-10 battle. On pure summit finishes the Dane continues to lag behind the elite GC climbers.

keyMoments.what: Dropped from the leading GC group before the Les Angles summit finish

riderPerformances.name: Lenny Martinez
riderPerformances.narrative: Martinez finishes 13th at +0:12, staying in contact with the top GC group through the summit. He sits 8th overall at +1:09 after the stage — a manageable gap for a rider with strong mountain instincts. He remains inside the top 10 and within range of a potential top-5 finish in Paris.

riderPerformances.name: Romain Grégoire
riderPerformances.narrative: A brutal day for Grégoire. The young Frenchman, promising in GC terms entering the Pyrenees, is dropped far from the summit and finishes 75th at +20:18 — well over 20 minutes adrift of the winner. The first true summit finish exposes the gap between his puncheur talents and the pure climbers leading the race. His GC ambitions are effectively ended by Stage 3.

riderPerformances.name: Thymen Arensman
riderPerformances.narrative: Arensman finishes 19th at +0:27 — a solid result from a Netcompany-INEOS domestique. He sits 16th in GC at +2:19 after the stage, slightly behind the top GC group but within the same peloton grouping. His climbing abilities keep him in the picture even if his primary job is protecting GC teammates.

riderPerformances.name: Mathieu van der Poel
riderPerformances.narrative: Stage 3's Pyrenean climbing is not van der Poel's terrain. He finishes 125th at +24:17 — deep in the gruppetto — which is entirely expected and accepted. Not a concern for his Tour ambitions; he remains Alpecin-Premier Tech's focal point for flatter stages and sprints ahead.

riderPerformances.name: Tom Pidcock
riderPerformances.narrative: Pidcock finishes 16th at +0:18, limiting his losses on a pure mountain stage. He sits 11th in GC at +1:22 after the stage — staying within range of the top 10 and keeping his options open for more suitable terrain ahead.

riderPerformances.name: Alex Baudin
riderPerformances.narrative: Baudin is the star of Stage 3's breakaway. The young Frenchman crests the Col de Toses summit first, claiming the polka-dot jersey and becoming the new King of the Mountains ahead of Alex Molenaar. He attacks again on the Col du Calvaire, extending his KOM lead at that summit. Only caught 11 km from the Les Angles finish as UAE's peloton reduces the gap to seconds — Baudin delivers one of the most aggressive breakaway performances of the early Tour, and earns his team a 3rd-place stage result via Carapaz.

keyMoments.what: Crests the Col de Toses summit first, takes the polka-dot jersey from Molenaar
Attacks on the Col du Calvaire, extends KOM lead at the summit
Caught by the peloton 11 km from the Les Angles finish

mechanicals.name: Egan Bernal
mechanicals.detail: Puncture on the Col de Toses descent forces Bernal to lose contact with the breakaway.

sources.title: Tour de France 2026: Pogacar strikes double in Les Angles
Official classifications of Tour de France 2026 — Stage 3
Tour de France : Tadej Pogacar remporte la 3e étape aux Angles
